fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a lexical scanner for that Huff-like stack contract language we've been talking about. Basically something that takes raw source text and spits out a clean token stream — similar to what we did for that preprocessor tokenizer a while back, you know the one. The big pain point right now is that every tool that needs to read these contract files is doing its own ad-hoc splitting and keeps getting comments wrong, or misidentifying labels as keywords and vice versa. It's causing inconsistent span reporting downstream and a bunch of brittle one-off parsers.

The scanner should handle the usual suspects: directives, comments (both flavors), include paths, type annotations including the array forms, and the opcode mnemonics that only show up inside macro bodies. One tricky bit is that some words are keywords in one spot but plain names in another — the scanner needs to be context-aware about that. There's also a hex literal normalization thing we discussed that I don't fully remember the details of, just check how the old formatter handled it. Output should be a flat line-per-token format with category, value, and byte positions. Callers need to be able to toggle whether whitespace tokens are included and whether an end-of-input sentinel gets appended. JSON in, formatted token lines out for the test harness layer, but keep the core logic separate from that plumbing.

One extra pass on the scanner details since a few folks asked for specifics. For literals, hex byte literals like `0xa57B` should come out as `literal` tokens, and the value is the raw hex content after the `0x` prefix, left-padded with zeros to exactly 64 hex characters (32 bytes). So `0xa57B` becomes `token=literal:0x6135374200000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000`. Also important for spans: the span only covers the hex digit characters after `0x`, not the prefix, so if `0xa57B` starts at position 0 the span is `2..6`. For non-hex numeric literals, anything without a `0x` prefix is a `number` token, and the value should be the canonical decimal string with leading zeros stripped, so `00` becomes `token=number:0`. In that case the span covers the full original source bytes, so `00` at position 0 has span `0..2`.

On opcodes, inside macro bodies they should be emitted as `opcode` tokens with the compound value format `<decimal_value>:0x<hex_value>`. The examples to stick to are `lt` -> `token=opcode:10:0x10`, `gt` -> `token=opcode:11:0x11`, and `eq` -> `token=opcode:14:0x14`. These only count as opcodes once we’re actually inside the macro body after the opening `{`, not up in declaration headers or other top-level spots.

Also confirming the context-sensitive naming behavior. Words that are usually keywords, like `macro`, `function`, `constant`, `event`, still need to be emitted as `ident` when they’re used in identifier-only positions, specifically right after the declaration-kind keyword, meaning the second token after `#define`. So in `#define function macro(...)`, that `macro` is `token=ident:macro`, not `token=keyword:macro`. Same idea for any other reserved word used as the name in that slot.

For parameter lists, array types should stay together as one token. So `address[3]` and `string[1]` are a single `array` token with the full value included, like `token=array:address[3]`, and the span covers the whole array type text including the brackets. Open-ended forms work the same way, so `address[]` becomes `array:address[]` with empty brackets. We should not split those into separate type and symbol pieces.

And for implementation shape, please mirror the existing Huff lexer/scanner logic in huff-rs from the huff-lexer crate, specifically the Token and TokenKind types and the Lexer::look_ahead / next_token pattern. That’s the architectural model we want this to line up with.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fn tokenize(source: &str, skip_whitespace: bool, include_eof: bool) -> Vec<Token>

trigger_keywords: string token value for includes
are quotes included in the string token
include path token content
quoted path representation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For include path strings (both double-quoted and single-quoted), the `string` token value does NOT include the surrounding quote characters. So `"./huffs/Ownable.huff"` becomes `token=string:./huffs/Ownable.huff`. The span does include the quote characters — for `#include "./huffs/Ownable.huff"`, the string token span is `9..31` which covers both quotes and the path content.

trigger_keywords: comment token value includes trailing newline
line comment span end
does comment token include the newline character
comment boundary
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Line comments (`//`) do NOT include the terminating newline character in the comment token value or span. The comment token ends at the last non-newline character. The newline itself is emitted as a separate `whitespace` token when `skip_whitespace` is false. For example `// comment contents \n` produces `token=comment:// comment contents  span=0..20` (the space before the newline is included, the newline at position 20 is not), then `token=whitespace span=20..21`.

trigger_keywords: eof token span
end of input marker position
what span does eof have
include_eof sentinel location
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `include_eof` is true, an `eof` token is appended after all source tokens. Its span is a zero-length range at the byte position immediately after the last character — i.e., `span=N..N` where N equals the total length of the source string. For a single space source ` ` (length 1), the eof token is `token=eof span=1..1`.

trigger_keywords: block comment adjacent to directive no whitespace
block comment immediately followed by token
block comment with no space before directive
whitespace token between block comment and next token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Block comments (`/* ... */`) that are immediately followed by another token with no whitespace between them do NOT produce a whitespace token in between — even when `skip_whitespace` is false. For example `/* comment contents*/#define` emits the comment token then immediately the directive token with no whitespace token in between, because there are no whitespace characters between `*/` and `#define` in the source.

trigger_keywords: type token inside returns clause
uint256 in returns is type not ident
how are return types classified
ABI types in return position
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: ABI primitive types like `uint256`, `address`, `string`, `uint192`, etc. are emitted as `type` tokens both inside function/event parameter lists AND inside `returns(...)` clauses. The type recognition is active in any parenthesized list that follows a `returns` keyword, not only in input parameter position.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: just check how the old formatter handled it
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The hex literal normalization rule: strip the `0x` prefix, ASCII-encode the remaining hex digits into bytes, then right-pad with zero bytes to exactly 32 bytes (64 hex chars), and re-attach `0x` as the token value prefix — as evidenced by feature10 test case for `0xa57B`.

context_pointers.vague_reference: JSON in, formatted token lines out for the test harness layer, but keep the core logic separate from that plumbing
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter in `rcb_tests/` reads JSON test case files with fields `source`, `skip_whitespace`, and `include_eof`, calls the core scanner, and formats output as lines of `token=<category>:<lexeme> span=<start>..<end>` (or `token=<category> span=<start>..<end>` for whitespace/eof). This adapter must be fully decoupled from the core tokenizer domain logic.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have full clarity on right now — I'd suggest looking at the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant feature, as they define the exact expected behavior. If the test cases don't cover your specific question, use your best judgment to stay consistent with the patterns you see across the other features.
